plan.summary: Identify and add appropriate accessible labels (e.g., `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby`) to 12 interactive elements within the `index.html` file to improve accessibility for screen reader users.

steps.description: Manually inspect the `index.html` file to locate the 12 interactive elements (e.g., `<button>`, `<a>`, `<input>`, `<select>`, `<textarea>`, or custom elements) that are missing accessible labels. Understand the context and intended purpose of each element to determine the most appropriate label.
steps.details: Use browser developer tools to identify interactive elements. Pay close attention to elements that perform actions or require user input but lack a visible or programmatic label. Document each instance found.

steps.description: For each of the 12 identified interactive elements in `index.html`, implement the most suitable accessible label. This typically involves adding an `aria-label` attribute with a concise, descriptive text if no visible label exists, or using `aria-labelledby` to reference the `id` of an existing visible label element. For interactive images, ensure the `alt` attribute is descriptive or the parent interactive element has an `aria-label`.
steps.details: Modify `index.html` by adding `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` attributes. Example: `<button aria-label="Close dialog">X</button>` or `<input type="text" aria-label="Search query">`.

steps.description: Perform local accessibility testing using automated tools to verify that the issue is resolved. Open the application in a web browser and run an accessibility audit.
steps.details: Use browser extensions like Lighthouse or Axe DevTools to re-scan the page. Confirm that all 12 instances of 'Interactive element missing accessible label' are no longer reported in `index.html`.

steps.description: Conduct manual testing using a screen reader to ensure the implemented labels are effective and provide a good user experience.
steps.details: Navigate through the interactive elements in `index.html` using a screen reader (e.g., NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver). Verify that the purpose of each element is clearly and accurately announced, and that the labels are not redundant or confusing.

steps.description: Submit the modified `index.html` for code review to ensure adherence to accessibility best practices and project standards.
steps.details: Request a peer review of the changes to `index.html` to confirm the accuracy, clarity, and conciseness of the `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` attributes.

code_preview.code: Read more

risks: Incorrect or ambiguous `aria-label` values could lead to misleading information for screen reader users.
Introducing redundant labels if an `aria-label` is added to an element that already has a visible label or is implicitly labeled, potentially creating a confusing experience for screen reader users.
Overlooking some of the 12 instances during manual inspection, leading to an incomplete resolution of the accessibility bug.
plan.rollback_strategy: Revert the changes made to `index.html` to the previous version using version control (e.g., `git checkout index.html`).
plan.success_criteria: All 12 instances of 'Interactive element missing accessible label' reported in `index.html` are resolved according to automated accessibility audit tools (e.g., Lighthouse, Axe DevTools). Manual screen reader testing confirms that all interactive elements in `index.html` are clearly and accurately labeled, providing a good user experience.
plan.original_request:
Fix this accessibility issue:

**Problem**: accessibility
**Description**: Interactive element missing accessible label (found in 1 file(s))
**Severity**: medium
**Affected files**: index.html
**Instances**: 12
